theorem addInterventionNodes_comm_hardIntervention (G : CDMG Node)
    (W₁ W₂ : Finset Node) (hW₁ : W₁ ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V) (hW₂ : W₂ ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V)
    (hDisj : Disjoint W₁ W₂) :
    (extendingCDMGsWith G W₁ hW₁).hardInterventionOn
        (W₂.image IntExtNode.unsplit)
        (image_unsplit_subset_extendingCDMGsWith_carrier hW₁ hW₂)
      = addInterventionNodesAndHardInterventionOn G W₁ W₂ hW₁ hW₂
      ∧
    extendingCDMGsWith (G.hardInterventionOn W₂ hW₂) W₁
        (subset_carrier_of_hardInterventionOn hW₂ hW₁)
      = addInterventionNodesAndHardInterventionOn G W₁ W₂ hW₁ hW₂
lean_blocks.explanation: This is the main theorem for sub-claim (b). It formalises the LN's triple coincidence $(G_{\doit(I_{W_1})})_{\doit(W_2)} = (G_{\doit(W_2)})_{\doit(I_{W_1})} = G_{\doit(I_{W_1}, W_2)}$ as a conjunction of two literal CDMG equalities, both with the mixed-notation `addInterventionNodesAndHardInterventionOn G W₁ W₂ hW₁ hW₂` as the shared right-hand side. Unlike sub-claim (a), literal `=` is available here because `hardInterventionOn` preserves the node carrier, so every CDMG in the chain lives in `CDMG (IntExtNode Node)` and there is no carrier mismatch to bridge via `eqViaNodeMap`. The first conjunct (b-1) is `rfl` by the definition of `addInterventionNodesAndHardInterventionOn` — it is stated only to preserve the LN's three-CDMG presentation in the signature and to parallel (a)'s shape, giving consumers a uniform `.1` / `.2` projection convention. The second conjunct (b-2) is the genuine content: that extending then hard-intervening agrees with hard-intervening then extending. `Disjoint W₁ W₂` is content-load-bearing here too — without it, a node in `W₁ ∩ W₂ ∩ (G.V \ G.J)` would acquire a fresh `.intCopy` on the LHS but land in the relabelled `J` on the middle term, breaking the equality. The outer constructors' carrier hypotheses are discharged by the two sibling helpers above.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Main theorem for sub-claim (b): two literal `=` conjuncts with the mixed-notation CDMG on the right.
theorem addInterventionNodes_comm_hardIntervention (G : CDMG Node)
    -- Same binder shape as (a) so consumers pass identical hypothesis lists to both theorems.
    (W₁ W₂ : Finset Node) (hW₁ : W₁ ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V) (hW₂ : W₂ ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V)
    (hDisj : Disjoint W₁ W₂) :
    -- (b-1) LHS: extend by `W₁` first, then hard-intervene on `W₂.image .unsplit` over the lifted carrier.
    (extendingCDMGsWith G W₁ hW₁).hardInterventionOn
        (W₂.image IntExtNode.unsplit)
        -- Outer `hardInterventionOn`'s carrier hypothesis: `W₂.image .unsplit ⊆ (extended).J ∪ (extended).V`.
        (image_unsplit_subset_extendingCDMGsWith_carrier hW₁ hW₂)
      -- (b-1) is `rfl` by the definition of `addInterventionNodesAndHardInterventionOn`.
      = addInterventionNodesAndHardInterventionOn G W₁ W₂ hW₁ hW₂
      ∧
    -- (b-2) middle: hard-intervene on `W₂` first, then extend by `W₁` over the hard-intervened carrier.
    extendingCDMGsWith (G.hardInterventionOn W₂ hW₂) W₁
        -- Inner `extendingCDMGsWith`'s carrier hypothesis via `subset_carrier_of_hardInterventionOn`.
        (subset_carrier_of_hardInterventionOn hW₂ hW₁)
      -- (b-2) is the genuine content of the LN's triple coincidence — commutativity of the two doit operations.
      = addInterventionNodesAndHardInterventionOn G W₁ W₂ hW₁ hW₂

addition_to_the_LN: [doit_overloaded_for_node_addition_vs_hard_intervention] The notation $G_{\doit(\cdot)}$ is overloaded in this lemma and is disambiguated by the type of its argument. For $W \subseteq J \cup V$, the expression $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ denotes the operation of adding the intervention nodes $I_W$ to $G$, whereas $G_{\doit(W)}$ denotes the standard hard intervention on $W$ (severing incoming edges into $W$). When both an intervention-node set and a pre-existing-node set appear together within a single $\doit(\cdot)$, as in $G_{\doit(I_{W_1}, W_2)}$, the notation denotes the composition of the two operations.
design_choices:
**Triple coincidence rendered as a conjunction with a shared right-hand side, not a raw $A = B = C$.** Lean has no native triple equality, so each sub-claim's $A = B = C$ is rewritten as $A = C \wedge B = C$. The LN's swap-symmetry reading $A = B$ is recovered by transitivity through the shared right-hand side ($G_{\doit(I_{W_1 \cup W_2})}$ for (a), the mixed-notation CDMG for (b)). This mirrors `claim_3_4` and `claim_3_7`, so consumers can call all three commute-style rows uniformly at `.1` / `.2`. A literal `iter₁₂ = iter₂₁` was rejected because, in (a), the two iterated extensions wrap the same abstract intervention symbol with different constructor combinations (`.unsplit (.intCopy w)` vs. `.intCopy (.unsplit w)`), so structural `=` is genuinely false.

**Sub-claim (a) uses `eqViaNodeMap` with `flattenIntExt`; sub-claim (b) uses literal `=`.** The two iterated extensions in (a) live in `CDMG (IntExtNode (IntExtNode Node))` while the joint single extension lives in `CDMG (IntExtNode Node)`, a strictly different Lean type — literal `=` is not even type-correct. The LN's set-theoretic identification of the two carriers via the canonical inclusion $\iota : J \cup V \hookrightarrow J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$ is realised as the flatten function `flattenIntExt`, applied field-wise to the four `Finset` data of the CDMG. In (b), by contrast, `hardInterventionOn` preserves the carrier, so every CDMG in the chain lives in `CDMG (IntExtNode Node)` and Lean's structural `=` carries the LN's "same CDMG" reading directly.

**Mixed-notation $G_{\doit(I_{W_1}, W_2)}$ defined as a composition `def`, not a fresh primitive.** The LN's `:=` introduces this notation as an abbreviation for $(G_{\doit(I_{W_1})})_{\doit(W_2)}$; the Lean `def` `addInterventionNodesAndHardInterventionOn` mirrors the equation literally. A field-by-field record-style construction was rejected because it would either need an extra equality to the composition or drift from the LN's own definition. Standalone (not a `let` inside the theorem) so downstream rows that mix intervention-node addition with hard intervention (do-calculus, iSCM intervention algebra) can reuse the same name.

**Two carrier-subset helpers, both `private`, both standalone.** `image_unsplit_subset_extendingCDMGsWith_carrier` discharges `W.image .unsplit ⊆ (extended).J ∪ (extended).V`; `subset_carrier_of_hardInterventionOn` discharges `W ⊆ (G.hardInterventionOn _ _).J ∪ (G.hardInterventionOn _ _).V`. Inlining either as a `by`-block in the theorem signature was rejected because the outer constructors demand proof terms and inlining clutters the rendered statement, and because each helper is used at multiple call sites. Both helpers are subset-transport (not set-equality) so the call site avoids a `Finset.Subset.trans` step. Neither consumes disjointness — well-typedness is disjointness-free across the row; disjointness enters only the content of the equalities.

**`Disjoint W₁ W₂` (Mathlib's `Finset.Disjoint`) over a raw `W₁ ∩ W₂ = ∅`.** The `Disjoint` form is destructed by `Finset.disjoint_left` / `Finset.disjoint_right`, which is what the field-level checks in the proof bodies consume directly. It is content-load-bearing in both sub-claims: in (a) the iterated construction would double-count any node in `W₁ ∩ W₂ ∩ G.V`; in (b) such a node would receive a fresh `.intCopy` on one side but land in the relabelled `J` on the other. The `linter.unusedVariables` exemption on (a)'s theorem reflects that the `Finset`-union `(W₁ \ G.J) ∪ (W₂ \ G.J) = (W₁ ∪ W₂) \ G.J` deduplicates without explicit appeal to `hDisj`, so the binder is kept for LN-faithfulness even though the proof body never names it.

**Carrier preconditions are `Wᵢ ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V`, not `Wᵢ ⊆ G.V`.** Matches `def_3_13`'s `extendingCDMGsWith` API and the LN's literal $W_i \subseteq J \cup V$. Overlap with `J` is admitted: by `def_3_13`'s convention $I_j := j$ for $j \in J$, the intervention-node addition reduces to the identity on the `J`-branch, and the lemma is vacuously true there. Reading `Wᵢ ⊆ G.V` would strictly weaken the lemma.

**`flattenIntExt` is a function, not an `Equiv`.** A type-level `IntExtNode (IntExtNode Node) ≃ IntExtNode Node` does not exist — the source has four reachable constructor combinations per node and the target two, so no bijection on the underlying types is possible. The map is injective only on the iterated carrier, and `Disjoint W₁ W₂` is exactly what makes the restriction well-defined. Image-level reasoning via `Finset.image flattenIntExt` is enough for (a)'s `eqViaNodeMap`. The off-carrier branch `.intCopy (.intCopy _) => .intCopy w` is a totality filler that is never reached on the iterated carrier.
